Purpose Delivery Interface

DWARF V3 June M2 delivery package

DWARF makes Cardano security work operational.

A clean, Docker-deliverable harness with a web graphical user interface (GUI) for scenario discovery, operator guidance, runtime status, and evidence-oriented workflows. This page is the polished recipient walkthrough for the scoped June M2 package.

Review the interface See deployment flow
28M2 scenarios rendered
5interface views captured
1containerized package
0external page assets

Why it matters

From scattered tests to a usable security substrate.

DWARF is positioned as the operating layer for adversarial Cardano testing: scenario inventory, repeatable deployment, built-in guidance, and evidence preservation all need to be visible to the person receiving the package.

Scenario substrate

Adversarial tests made navigable

DWARF packages a large scenario catalog behind an operator graphical user interface so security review work starts from a visible inventory.

Delivery discipline

Install, deploy, undeploy

The June M2 package is structured for a recipient-owned Linux box with scripts, Docker packaging, and operational docs.

Evidence loop

Runs can become artifacts

The workflow emphasizes preserving bundles and writing assertions, not just clicking through a demo surface.

Static handoff

This page travels cleanly

The walkthrough is a single HyperText Markup Language (HTML) object with embedded June M2 screenshots, suitable for bench review now and later static hosting.

Delivery model

Built to land on a recipient-owned Linux box.

The current package is intended to be handed off as a clean repository or archive, installed with the included scripts, deployed as Docker infrastructure, and validated from the browser.

1

Install

Prepare host dependencies and package directories using the install script and documented Linux prerequisites.

2

Deploy

Build or load the DWARF image, start the service, and wait for the status endpoint before handing over the graphical interface.

3

Operate

Use Operate, Scenarios, Learn, Getting Started, and Status to run the first review workflow.

4

Preserve

Keep run output and bundles as evidence so results can be reviewed outside the live container.

Recipient command shape

Simple enough to verify, explicit enough to audit.

The package should remain boring at the infrastructure layer: install, deploy, check status, operate, then undeploy when done.

./delivery/scripts/install.sh
./delivery/scripts/deploy.sh
curl http://127.0.0.1:8787/api/status
open http://127.0.0.1:8787
./delivery/scripts/undeploy.sh

Graphical user interface walkthrough

The user-facing surface after deployment.

These screenshots were captured from the scoped DWARF June M2 deployment through Playwright. They show what a recipient should be able to reach after the container is running.

01 / Operator Entry

Operate starts with a task-oriented console.

The landing page gives a recipient a direct place to run, inspect, and preserve DWARF security work instead of hunting through scripts or raw folders.

Use this view as the operator handoff point after deployment.

02 / Scenario Inventory

28 scoped M2 scenarios are visible in the shipped catalog.

The catalog exposes the M2 serialization/deserialization and first-execution scope without carrying later scenario families into this delivery.

Delivery smoke test rendered the catalog with 28 scenarios.

03 / Built-In Guidance

Learn keeps operator documentation inside the graphical interface.

Recipients do not need to cross-reference chat logs to begin. The web app surfaces the documentation map directly in the same interface they use to operate DWARF.

Use Learn immediately after first deploy for orientation.

04 / First Run Path

Getting Started turns installation into a first evidence workflow.

The page shows the practical operator path: install, deploy, run a scenario, find the run, preserve a bundle, and write an assertion.

This is the expected recipient walkthrough for a new Linux box.

05 / Runtime Health

Status gives the quick readiness check.

The status view provides a lightweight confirmation that the web surface is reachable and the deployed package is behaving as an operator-facing application.

Smoke verification used /api/status and browser rendering.
DWARF V3 June M2 delivery walkthrough · self-contained HTML with embedded Playwright screenshots · prepared for bench review and later static hosting.
